# Modify CI relationships

**Connector:** [SDP - CMDB (v1 legacy)](../README.md) (`sdp-cmdb-v1`) · **Action index:** [all actions](README.md)
**Endpoint:** `POST /api/cmdb/cirelationships` · **Operation ID:** `ModifyCIRelationships`

Adds **or** deletes CI relationships — the verb lives inside the XML payload, not the URL (legacy V1 API).

## Parameters

| Designer label | Name | Where | Type | Required |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Input Data (XML) | `INPUT_DATA` | form field | string | yes |

### Input Data (XML) (`INPUT_DATA`)

The XML relationship document — full templates below.

## The `INPUT_DATA` payload — XML, not JSON

This is the one action in the family that takes **XML** (the V1 API predates the JSON
envelope). It travels as a form field; paste the document as one line or with line breaks —
both survive form-encoding.

### Formatting rules

- Keep the `<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>` declaration.
- Escape XML specials inside CI names / values: `&` → `&amp;`, `<` → `&lt;`, `>` → `&gt;`.
- `relationshiptype` values must match your instance's relationship-type catalogue
  (via the Query connector, `templates/05-relationship-types.sql` lists them).
- CI names are **case-sensitive**, as everywhere in V1.

### Variation 1 — add a relationship

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<API version="1.0" locale="en">
  <records><relationships>
    <addrelationship>
      <toci>CI-A</toci>
      <relationshiptype>Depends on</relationshiptype>
      <relatedcis>
        <citype>Windows Server</citype>
        <ci><name>CI-B</name></ci>
      </relatedcis>
    </addrelationship>
  </relationships></records>
</API>
```

### Variation 2 — delete a relationship

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<API version="1.0" locale="en">
  <relationships>
    <deleterelationship>
      <fromci>CI-A</fromci>
      <relationshiptype>Depends on</relationshiptype>
      <relatedcis><ci>CI-B</ci></relatedcis>
    </deleterelationship>
  </relationships>
</API>
```

Note the asymmetry (it is the API's, not a typo): **add** nests under
`<records><relationships>` and names the far end `<toci>`; **delete** skips `<records>` and
uses `<fromci>`.

## Authentication

The connection sends the technician key on every call as the `TECHNICIAN_KEY` **query
parameter** (secured connection parameter; same secret value as the V3 `authtoken`). Because
it travels in the URL, **HTTPS is mandatory** and the key should be scoped tightly — see the
[connector README](../README.md#whats-different-about-v1).

## Response

Success (200) returns the `V1Envelope` shape:

- `API` — object

V1 responses are JSON with the legacy envelope
`{ "API": { "response": { "operation": { "result": {...}, "Details": ... } } } }`.
Success is `result.statuscode` **200** (with `result.status` `"Success"`); errors put the
reason in `result.message`. This is *not* the V3 `response_status` shape.

Anything else comes back on the `default` response — V1 failures put the reason in `result.message`, and V3-style rejections can also appear.

## Notes, limits & verification

- Modeled from a working V1 client and the official V1↔V3 comparison; live-verify on a sub-15100 instance before relying on it (on 15100+ builds, like the demo, V3 supersedes this endpoint).
- Write operation — test on a disposable instance only, never on the read-only public demo.
